results.seo_title: Stable Audio 3: Fast Latent Diffusion Models for Audio Generation…
results.meta_description: Stability AI releases Stable Audio 3, a family of fast latent diffusion models for audio generation and editing, with improved performance on benchmark tests.
results.body_html:
Stability AI has released Stable Audio 3, a family of latent diffusion models for instrumental music and sound effects generation.

Small runs on a MacBook Pro M4 CPU Medium fits on consumer GPUs with 8 GB of VRAM Generates stereo audio at 44.1 kHz using a three-stage training pipeline

BBC Sound Effects benchmark at 5 seconds FAD 0.369 (lower than every open-weight baseline)

Advantages Over Prior Models Our latent diffusion model (LDM)-based approach outperforms the state-of-the-art AudioLDM on most metrics and stays comparable on the rest on AudioCaps test set, despite training the LDM on a 63 times smaller dataset and keeping the text encoder frozen.

Availability of Open-Weight Variants

Small variants available with open weights Medium variant available with open weights

Data Leakage in Cross-Modal Retrieval Training: A Case Study OpenAlex (Text-to-Audio Generation using Instruction Guided Latent Diffusion Model)

source.title: Stability AI Releases Stable Audio 3: A Family of Fast Latent Diffusion Models for Audio Generation and Editing
source.summary:
Here is a summary of the news item in 2-4 sentences:

Stability AI has released Stable Audio 3, a family of latent diffusion models for generating and editing instrumental music and sound effects. The models are designed to be fast and efficient, with open weights available for smaller variants that can run on consumer GPUs. According to benchmark results, the medium variant of SA3 outperforms other open-weight baselines, scoring lower than all of them on the BBC Sound Effects benchmark.

results.seo_title: Proactive Goal-Directed Intelligence via Composable Sandboxed Programs
results.meta_description: A new AI architecture that enables proactive goal-directed agents, advancing shared tasks without waiting for user prompts.
results.body_html:
The new AI architecture, Context, enables proactive goal-directed agents that advance shared tasks without waiting for user prompts.

Key Components

  • Write-time context assembly precomputes enriched typed attributes via Groker agents
  • Composable sandboxed wisdom programs form a governed library of LM-generated imperative programs
  • Proactive goal stream state machines drive conversations toward terminal states
  • Context blocks are byte-identical across turns between semantic changes, enabling near-100% KV-cache reuse

Formal Results

  • The Context Stability Theorem: bounding per-turn LM cost as a function of semantic change rate
  • A Program Composition Correctness Theorem
  • A Declarative Wiring Soundness Theorem
  • The Proactive Dominance Theorem, proving proactive agents weakly dominate reactive agents on expected turns-to-terminal-state
  • Coordination Overhead Elimination and Quality Preservation, establishing Pareto improvements in multi-participant goal chats
  • A Cross-Platform Vote Consistency Theorem

Implementation

  • Implemented in the open-source Qbix / Safebox / Safebots stack

Related Research

  • A new method using nucleic acid secondary structure to assess phylogenetic relationships among species, known as molecular morphometrics

Future Work

  • Further research is needed to explore the applications of Context in various domains

Read time: 10 minutes

source.title: Context: Proactive Goal-Directed Intelligence via Composable Sandboxed Programs, Declarative Wiring, and Structured Interaction
source.summary: Researchers have introduced Context, an intelligence layer for the Magarshak Architecture that enables proactive goal-directed agents to advance shared tasks without waiting for user prompts. This architecture relies on three mechanisms: precomputing interaction context, composable sandboxed wisdom programs, and structured interaction content generation. The system has been formally proven to achieve stability, correctness, and dominance over reactive chatbots in expected turns-to-terminal-state.

results.seo_title: Quantum Frog: Emergent Cooperation and Difficulty Scaling in a…
results.meta_description: A new cooperative game Quantum Frog uses reinforcement learning to answer design questions about game difficulty, optimal policies, cooperation gap, and joint…
results.body_html:
The optimal strategy for Quantum Frog is a rush strategy, as it minimizes time exposure to traffic. This is universally optimal due to the quantized-time mechanic.

Game Difficulty Scaling with Traffic Density

Adding an uncoordinated second player is harder than sextupling the traffic for a single expert player. Traffic density has a significant impact on game difficulty, with higher densities making it more challenging. The quantized-time mechanic makes the game universally optimal, as time exposure to traffic is minimized.

Optimal Single-Agent Policy and Cooperation Gap

The optimal single-agent policy for Quantum Frog is a rush strategy, which minimizes time exposure to traffic. Cooperative training recovers +32--34 percentage points of joint success rate relative to independent agents. The cooperation gap between independent and cooperative two-agent play is significant, but can be reduced through cooperative training.

Emergent Cooperative Strategy and Joint Success Rate

The emergent cooperative strategy for Quantum Frog is synchronised rushing, not complex positional coordination. Cooperative training recovers +32--34 percentage points of joint success rate relative to independent agents. The joint strategy emerges when agents are incentivised to cooperate, and is synchronised rushing.

These findings provide concrete, empirically grounded guidance for the commercial design of Quantum Frog. The role of environment mechanics in shaping multi-agent learning dynamics is an area of future research. Further studies on the application of reinforcement learning to cooperative games are needed.

source.title: Quantum Frog: Emergent Cooperation and Difficulty Scaling in a Quantized-Time Cooperative Game
source.summary: Researchers have created a two-player cooperative game called "Quantum Frog" that requires players to work together to cross an 8x8 grid while avoiding traffic. The game uses a novel "quantized-time" mechanic, where the environment advances only when a player acts, and explores how different strategies emerge in response to varying levels of traffic density. Through reinforcement learning and experimentation with various training methods, the study found that a simple "rush strategy" is universally optimal, while cooperative play can significantly improve joint success rates and reduce episode length.

results.seo_title: Governance Horizon for Ethical-Use Constraints in Open-Weight AI…
results.meta_description: A governance horizon for ethical-use constraints in open-weight AI models is established through voluntary metadata disclosures and platform-level…
results.body_html:
The governance horizon for ethical-use constraints in open-weight AI models is determined by the half-life of restriction evidence, which decays with a half-life of 1.31 derivation steps.

Restriction evidence decays with a half-life of 1.31 derivation steps (R^2=0.98). Beyond seven downstream generations, at least 80% of descendant models lack sufficient public evidence for governance determination. Platform-level interventions reveal that policy design is the binding factor in governance infrastructure.

Orphan components Lineages with no inheritable upstream intent remain undecidable under any inheritance-only policy.

Unresolved upstream nodes Direct downstream undecidability bottlenecks that inheritance rules alone cannot recover.

Comparison with PyPI Comparison with PyPI corroborates that the collapse is topology-specific to open-weight derivation rather than inherent to open ecosystems.

The governance horizon for ethical-use constraints in open-weight AI models has a shallow, structurally determined reach. Achieving deep supply-chain accountability requires provenance mechanisms propagating governance signals through derivation itself.

source.title: A governance horizon for ethical-use constraints in open-weight AI models
source.summary: Researchers have audited 2.1 million model repositories on Hugging Face Hub to test the effectiveness of voluntary metadata disclosures as a governance framework for open-weight AI models. The study found that this approach has a limited reach, with only about 80% of descendant models having sufficient public evidence for governance determination after seven downstream generations. Platform-level interventions revealed that policy design is more important than enforcement in sustaining governance signals across model lineages. The results suggest that disclosure-based governance has a shallow and structurally determined limit in open-weight AI systems.

results.seo_title: ActQuant: Revolutionizing Vision-Language-Action Models with…
results.meta_description: Discover ActQuant, an action-guided mixed-precision quantization framework that enables efficient deployment of vision-language-action models on edge platforms.
results.body_html:
ActQuant enables efficient deployment of vision-language-action models on edge platforms with sub-4-bit quantization.

Retains 95.0% performance on OpenVLA-OFT and 94.8% on π_0.5 Compresses backbone from 14.3 GB to 2.7 GB (5.3×) Reduces memory footprint by 2.5×

Inter-tensor bit allocator assigns each weight matrix a single bit-width based on its contribution to predicting the agent's actions. Intra-tensor scale optimizer tunes per-block quantization scales using action-aware curvature, concentrating dynamic range on influential weights for control.

Hardware Profiling and Activation Analysis

Introduces a basic activation quantization strategy to balance task performance and hardware efficiency.

Pushes ActQuant to 2.5 bpw at 90.1% on OpenVLA-OFT, outperforming existing methods.

ActQuant enables efficient deployment of vision-language-action models on edge platforms with sub-4-bit quantization, offering significant performance gains and hardware efficiency improvements.

source.title: ActQuant: Sub-4-bit Action-Guided Quantization for Vision-Language-Action Models
source.summary: Researchers have introduced ActQuant, a new framework for sub-4-bit weight quantization in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models. The framework operates in two stages and uses an action-aware curvature to optimize per-block quantization scales, allowing for aggressive quantization while maintaining performance. ActQuant has been evaluated on both simulation and real-world hardware, achieving significant compression of model weights from 14.3 GB to 2.7 GB without sacrificing performance.

results.seo_title: Together AI Open-Sources OSCAR: An Attention-Aware 2-Bit KV Cache…
results.meta_description: Together AI has released OSCAR, an INT2 KV cache quantization method for long-context LLM serving. Learn how it reduces memory usage and improves decode…
results.body_html:
OSCAR reduces the BF16 accuracy gap to 3.78 points on Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507 and 1.42 points on Qwen3-8B, while delivering approximately 8× KV memory reduction and up to 3× decode speedup at 100K context length.

Key Benefits of OSCARReduces BF16 accuracy gap by 3.78 points on Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507Delivers approximately 8× KV memory reductionImproves decode speedup by up to 3× at 100K context lengthComparison with Existing MethodsMethodBF16 Accuracy GapKV Memory ReductionDecode SpeedupHadamard TransformsDegrades at INT2 due to misalignment with downstream attentionN/AN/AOSCAR3.78 points on Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507, 1.42 points on Qwen3-8B8× KV memory reduction, up to 3× decode speedup at 100K context lengthTheoretical Justification of OSCAROSCAR estimates attention-aware covariance structures offline and uses them to derive fixed rotations and clipping thresholds for quantization.Deployment and IntegrationSeamless integration into modern LLM serving

frameworks such as SGLangPaged KV-cache serving and fused kernel pipelines remain compatible with OSCAR system

source.title: Together AI Open-Sources OSCAR: An Attention-Aware 2-Bit KV Cache Quantization System for Long-Context LLM Serving
source.summary:
Here is a summary of the news item in 3 sentences:

Together AI has open-sourced OSCAR, an attention-aware 2-bit KV cache quantization system for long-context LLM serving. The method reduces the accuracy gap between BF16 and its quantized version by 3.78 points on certain benchmarks, while also achieving significant memory reduction (8x) and decode speedup (up to 3x). OSCAR is distinct from prior rotation-based approaches that apply data-oblivious Hadamard transforms, instead deriving separate rotations for keys and values based on attention-aware covariance structures.

results.seo_title: AutoResearch AI: Towards AI-Powered Research Automation for Scientific
results.meta_description: Scientific research is being reshaped by AI systems that move beyond isolated assistance toward longer-horizon workflows spanning literature grounding,…
results.body_html:
Scientific research is being reshaped by AI systems that move beyond isolated assistance toward longer-horizon workflows spanning literature grounding, hypothesis generation, experimentation, validation, reporting, and revision. This shift marks a transition from task-level AI for science to workflow-level research automation. Yet current systems remain fragmented, differing in autonomy, domain scope, execution environment, validation mechanism, and human oversight, while still struggling with evidence preservation, reproducibility, weak-direction rejection, provenance tracking, cross-domain robustness, and accountable scientific closure.

This survey examines these developments through AutoResearch, defined as the developmental spectrum of AI-powered scientific workflow automation. Within it, Vibe Research denotes the human-steered region of prompt-based assistance and human-verified execution, whereas emerging AI-led systems coordinate larger portions of the discovery loop without achieving robust autonomy. We analyze how research systems redistribute control, evidence, execution, validation, and accountability across workflows and organize the field around five workflow conditions: literature and research grounding; hypothesis formation and planning; experimentation and tool use; feedback, validation, and review; and reporting and knowledge communication.

We further synthesize AI scientist systems, mixed-initiative co-research frameworks, benchmarks, domain deployments, and open-source infrastructures. Finally, we propose five evaluation dimensions--novelty, validity, impact, reliability, and provenance--and show that AutoResearch autonomy is domain-conditioned, being more credible in structured, executable, and rapidly verifiable settings but limited in embodied, delayed, heterogeneous, ethical, or institutionally accountable contexts.
target_keywords: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation
trending_keywords_used: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: AutoResearch AI: Towards AI-Powered Research Automation for Scientific Discovery
source.summary: Researchers are developing AI systems to automate scientific discovery workflows, moving beyond isolated assistance towards a more comprehensive approach that incorporates literature grounding, hypothesis generation, experimentation, validation, reporting, and revision. However, current systems face challenges such as evidence preservation, reproducibility, and accountability, and vary in their autonomy, domain scope, and execution environment. A new framework called AutoResearch aims to address these issues by analyzing the development of AI-powered scientific workflow automation across various workflows and conditions. The framework proposes five evaluation dimensions to assess the credibility of AI systems in different domains.

results.seo_title: Ontological Knowledge Blocks for Trustworthy AI Systems
results.meta_description: A programmable governance infrastructure that compiles regulatory obligations into machine-checkable constraints over structured evidence graphs.
results.body_html:
AI-enabled services deployed in critical digital infrastructure are subject to governance obligations that remain documentation-centric, but a new programmable governance infrastructure called Ontological Knowledge Blocks (OKBs) compiles regulatory obligations into machine-checkable constraints over structured evidence graphs.

Executable Compliance: A deterministic regulatory compiler translates structured Intermediate Representation (IR) records into composable KB modules, enabling profile-based governance reconfiguration without modifying service code.

Normative Obligations RDF/OWL Concept Schema Executable SHACL Validation Rules Explicit Evidence Requirements PROV-O Provenance Links

Binds normative obligations to an RDF/OWL concept schema Executable SHACL validation rules Explicit evidence requirements PROV-O provenance links

Results Demonstrate: Profile-sensitive validation, strictly additive violation accumulation, SHACL validation latency between 12.6 ms and 100.3 ms, and profile equivalence testing confirming Combined as the strictly most comprehensive profile.

Implementation and Evaluation: Two prototypes implemented and evaluated in an AI-assisted HPC resource allocation scenario across 24 validation runs and four governance profiles.

Open Source Release: All artefacts released as open source, enabling further development and adoption of OKBs.
trending_keywords_used: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: Ontological Knowledge Blocks: Executable Compliance and Profile-Based Validation for Trustworthy AI Systems
source.summary: Researchers have introduced Ontological Knowledge Blocks (OKBs), a programmable governance infrastructure that compiles regulatory obligations into machine-checkable constraints for trustworthy AI systems. OKBs bind normative obligations to structured evidence graphs and enable profile-based governance reconfiguration without modifying service code. The system was evaluated in an AI-assisted HPC resource allocation scenario, demonstrating profile-sensitive validation and strict violation accumulation. The open-source artefacts of the OKB implementation were released.

results.seo_title: Co-ReAct: A Rubric-Guided Action-Selection Framework for ReAct Agents
results.meta_description: Co-ReAct improves ReAct agents' performance by injecting rubrics as step-level guidance during inference, consistently outperforming baselines on…
results.body_html:
Co-ReAct improves ReAct agents' performance by injecting rubrics as step-level guidance during inference, consistently outperforming baselines on DeepResearchBench and SQA-CS-V2.

Improved performance over ReAct agents on search-intensive tasks Consistent improvement across different baselines on DeepResearchBench and SQA-CS-V2 Step-level guidance during inference using rubrics Dedicated rubric generator with GRPO for reliable guidance

Rubric Generator Trained with GRPO for reliable guidance

Objective Function List-wise Spearman rank-correlation reward against multi-judge expert consensus rankings

Comparison to Existing Approaches Unlike prior pairwise or binary preference formulations, Co-ReAct optimizes a list-wise Spearman rank-correlation reward against multi-judge expert consensus rankings.

Co-ReAct consistently improves over ReAct agents across different baselines The trained rubric generator can serve as a drop-in component to improve these baselines without changing their underlying decision mechanisms
target_keywords: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: Co-ReAct: Rubrics as Step-Level Collaborators for ReAct Agents
source.summary: Researchers have developed Co-ReAct, a framework that uses rubrics to guide the reasoning process of ReAct-style agents on search-intensive tasks. Unlike previous approaches, Co-ReAct injects rubrics into the agent's context at each decision step to provide step-level guidance, improving the quality and efficiency of the agent's trajectories. The framework has been tested on two benchmark datasets and shown consistent improvements over existing baselines, including those using open-source and closed-source models.

results.seo_title: Do Vision-Language Benchmarks Really Test Vision?
results.meta_description: A study investigates the effectiveness of vision-language benchmarks in evaluating visual grounding in models.
results.body_html:
Do vision-language benchmarks really test vision? Benchmark accuracy is often implicitly assumed to reflect grounded visual understanding in vision-language models (VLMs), yet it remains unclear to what extent such scores truly reflect reliance on visual evidence.

Removing a substantial fraction of image tokens only degrades model performance very slightly on a widely used hallucination benchmark. Our analysis spans multiple levels of granularity, spanning global visual degradation.

Question ReformulationAnswer-Space ExpansionDecision-Level Analyses

Global visual degradation has a minimal impact on model performance. Localized occlusion also shows little effect on model predictions. Even when the final prediction remains unchanged, the model's internal support for the correct answer may already be weakened.

Layer-Wise Analysis of Vision-Token Geometry Increasing similarity among visual tokens in deeper layers provides a possible explanation for our findings.

Current benchmarks are not sufficient to reliably evaluate fine-grained visual grounding in VLMs. Our results suggest that reliance on visual evidence is less critical than previously thought.

Further research is needed to develop more effective benchmarks for evaluating visual grounding in VLMs. A framework for semantic drift and collapse could provide valuable insights into the limitations of current models.
target_keywords: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation
trending_keywords_used: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: Seeing without Looking: Do Vision-Language Benchmarks Really Test Vision?
source.summary: Researchers have found that vision-language models (VLMs) do incorporate visual input, but their predictions remain relatively unchanged even when the model is deprived of fine-grained visual evidence. This suggests that standard benchmark accuracy may not be a reliable measure of a VLM's ability to understand visual information. The findings imply that current benchmarks are insufficient for evaluating the fine-grained visual grounding in VLMs.

results.seo_title: A Fine-Tuned BERT Classifier for Personal-Letter Titles in Late-Ming…
results.meta_description: A fine-tuned BERT classifier predicts personal letters in Classical Chinese wenji table of contents with high accuracy, benefiting the China Biographical…
results.body_html:
Direct answer: A fine-tuned BERT classifier has been developed to predict personal letters in Classical Chinese wenji table of contents with high accuracy.

The model was deployed on Hugging Face and used at the China Biographical Database (CBDB) to identify approximately fifty-five thousand letters across mid-Ming through early-Qing wenji, populating the Ming Letter Platform. The CBDB has a relational database of approximately 360,000 individuals up until April 2015.

The BERT classifier was fine-tuned on 5438 hand-labeled wenji titles from thirty-three late-Ming and early-Qing literati. The model achieved high accuracy in predicting personal letters.

The use of the fine-tuned BERT classifier has improved the accuracy of letter identification in the CBDB. The project has contributed to the development of digital humanities collaborations and new forms of analysis.

The model uses a scaling-aware adapter for efficient inference. The triple-component attention profiling mechanism improves the accuracy of letter prediction.

Further research is needed to explore the potential applications of the fine-tuned BERT classifier in other domains. The model's performance may be affected by the quality of the training data.
target_keywords: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation
trending_keywords_used: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: A Fine-Tuned BERT Classifier for Personal-Letter Titles in Late-Ming and Early-Qing Collected Works
source.summary: A researcher has developed a fine-tuned BERT classifier called Lepton that can predict whether a title in Classical Chinese texts is a personal letter or a preface. The model was trained on 5,438 hand-labeled titles from late-Ming and early-Qing literati and has been used to identify approximately 55,000 letters across a range of texts. This classifier has been deployed at the China Biographical Database (CBDB) to populate the Ming Letter Platform.

46 Representative Architectures 8 Categories 96.2% of HuggingFace Transformers architectures covered

Architecture Family Number of Kernels

Aggregate Speedup Over Production Baselines: 0.94x

FastKernels doubles as a minimalistic, production-grade inference framework Runs at parity with hardened systems like vLLM and SGLang on mainstream LLM serving Substantially exceeds upstream references on under-served architectures

Agent Performance Speedup Over Production Baselines

FastKernels is a stepping stone toward kernel agents whose benchmark gains translate directly into production throughput improvements. Code available at https://github.com/Snowflake-AI-Research/fastkernels

source.title: FastKernels: Benchmarking GPU Kernel Generation in Production
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new benchmark, FastKernels, to evaluate GPU kernel generation in production environments. The benchmark consists of 46 representative architectures and aims to address the limitations of existing benchmarks by mirroring the interface and compilation stack of real systems. Evaluating state-of-the-art kernel agents on FastKernels, researchers found that even the strongest agent achieved only a 0.94x speedup over production baselines, highlighting the need for more accurate benchmarking in the field.

results.seo_title: Goal-Conditioned Agents that Learn Everything All at Once: A…
results.meta_description: A new approach to reinforcement learning allows agents to learn everything all at once, achieving significant speed-ups and outperforming existing methods.
results.body_html:
Direct answer: A new approach to reinforcement learning allows agents to learn everything all at once, achieving significant speed-ups and outperforming existing methods.

Key Benefits of Learning Everything All at Once

  • Significant speed-up compared to traditional all-goals relabelling (< 250x)
  • Outperforms existing methods on goal-conditioned Craftax
  • Competitive with existing baselines on continuous control environments

How it Works

Learning Everything All at Once (LEO) jointly outputs values and actions for every goal at once, allowing for efficient, parallel all-goals updates with a single pass through the network.

Key Components
Joint output of values and actions for every goal
Efficient, parallel all-goals updates
Single pass through the network

Potential Applications

Unlocking all-goals learning at scale can serve as a useful tool for RL practitioners in complex environments.

  • Complex environments
  • RL practitioners
  • Scalable solutions

Future Directions

Using LEO as a teacher network, rather than a direct actor, can further enhance its power.

  • Teacher network approach
  • Enhanced performance
  • Further research needed
target_keywords: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation
trending_keywords_used: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: Goal-Conditioned Agents that Learn Everything All at Once
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new approach to reinforcement learning called Learning Everything All at Once (LEO), which allows goal-conditioned agents to learn from every transition in an environment simultaneously and efficiently. This method, which jointly outputs values and actions for multiple goals, significantly outperforms existing methods on certain tasks while achieving a substantial speed-up. LEO has the potential to unlock all-goals learning at scale, making it a useful tool for reinforcement learning practitioners in complex environments. The approach has been open-sourced.

results.seo_title: QED: An Open-Source Multi-Agent System for Generating Mathematical…
results.meta_description: An open-source multi-agent system that generates complete mathematical proofs without human guidance, leveraging domain expertise and robust pipelines.
results.body_html:
QED generates complete mathematical proofs without human guidance, leveraging domain expertise and robust pipelines.

Decomposition agent structures the proof search Prover agents generate candidate arguments Verifier agents check correctness

Decomposition agent Structures the proof search

Prover agents Generate candidate arguments

QED produced five original works across algebraic geometry, fluid PDEs, probability, and inverse problems. Expert assessments regard these works as solid specialized research contributions.

Fifth Infra Spoke: Release and Collaboration

QED is released at https://github.com/proofQED/QED. The system was evaluated in collaboration with domain experts on 18 research-level projects of varying difficulty.

source.title: QED: An Open-Source Multi-Agent System for Generating Mathematical Proofs on Open Problems
source.summary: Researchers have developed an open-source multi-agent system called QED that can generate complete mathematical proofs for open problems without human guidance. The system, which separates planning, proving, and verification tasks, was evaluated on 18 research-level projects and produced five original works in various fields of mathematics. These works were assessed by experts as solid contributions to the field, comparable in difficulty and scope to published work in specialist mathematics venues. QED is now available for public use at its GitHub repository.

results.seo_title: Improving Hugging Face Model Access for Kaggle Users
results.meta_description: Kaggle and Hugging Face users can now access Hugging Face models directly on Kaggle, enhancing visibility and discoverability.
results.body_html:
Kaggle and Hugging Face users can now access Hugging Face models directly on Kaggle, enhancing visibility and discoverability.

You can navigate from Hugging Face models to Kaggle and vice versa by visiting a Hugging Face model page like Qwen/Qwen3-1.7B. To use it in a Kaggle Notebook, click on “Use this model” and select “Kaggle” to open up a Kaggle notebook with a pre-populated code snippet to load the model. You can do the same from a Hugging Face model page on Kaggle by clicking the “Code” button.

If you use a private Hugging Face model in your Kaggle notebook, authenticate via your Hugging Face account as normal (add your HF_TOKEN in the “Add-ons > Secrets” menu in the notebook editor). A Hugging Face model page won’t be generated on Kaggle.

We’re actively working on a solution to seamlessly use Hugging Face models in Kaggle competitions that require offline notebook submissions. While this will take a few more months to complete, we believe the wait will be worth it.

Discover Hugging Face models and explore all the community examples in public notebooks in one place on Kaggle at https://www.kaggle.com/models. As more Hugging Face models are used on Kaggle, the number of models and associated code examples you can explore for inspiration will grow.

When browsing Hugging Face models on Kaggle, we want to make it easy for you to navigate back to Hugging Face to explore additional details, metadata, community usage in Hugging Face Spaces, discussion, and more. Simply click “Open in Hugging Face” on the Kaggle model page.

The integration uses a web agent framework to enable seamless access to Hugging Face models on Kaggle. The framework allows for secure distribution of models to different facilities, such as healthcare organizations.

source.title: Improving Hugging Face Model Access for Kaggle Users
source.summary: Improving Hugging Face Model Access for Kaggle Users

results.body_html:
Co-located vLLM in TRL enables efficient training and inference on shared GPUs, reducing idle time and boosting overall efficiency.

Unified Execution Skip HTTP Communication Torch Distributed Process Group Native Python Calls

GPU UtilizationIdle Time ReductionThroughput Increase Shared GPUsReduced idle time for training and inferenceBoosted overall efficiency

Improved GPU utilization Increased throughput Reduced idle time for training and inference

Implementation Notes External Launcher Distributed Executor Backend

Co-located vLLM respects the torch.distributed process group and rank structure. The co-located vLLM no longer relies on REST APIs, it runs directly in memory and communicates via native Python calls.

results.seo_title: Learn the Hugging Face Kernel Hub in 5 Minutes
results.meta_description: Discover how to use the Hugging Face Kernel Hub to boost model performance with pre-optimized kernels, easily loaded from the Hub.
results.body_html:
The Hugging Face Kernel Hub allows Python libraries and applications to load optimized compute kernels directly from the Hugging Face Hub.

Allows Python libraries and applications to load optimized compute kernels directly from the Hugging Face Hub. Think of it like the Model Hub, but for low-level, high-performance code snippets (kernels) that accelerate specific operations, often on GPUs.

Instant Access to Optimized Kernels: Load and run kernels optimized for various hardware starting with NVIDIA and AMD GPUs, without local compilation hassles. Share and Reuse: Discover, share, and reuse kernels across different projects and the community. Easy Updates: Stay up-to-date with the latest kernel improvements simply by pulling the latest version from the Hub. Accelerate Development: Focus on your model architecture and logic, not on the intricacies of kernel compilation and deployment.

How to Use the Kernel Hub (Basic Example)

Import get_kernel: This function is the entry point to the Kernel Hub via the kernels library. get_kernel(

results.body_html:
Featherless AI is now a supported Inference Provider on Hugging Face Hub, enhancing serverless inference capabilities.

Wide variety of text and conversational models, including latest open-source models from DeepSeek, Meta, Google, Qwen, and much more. Exceptionally large catalog of models available for users due to unique model loading and GPU orchestration abilities.

LanguageSDK Pythonhuggingface_hub JavaScript@huggingface/inference

Billing and Pricing Providers often offer either a low cost of access to a limited set of models, or an unlimited range of models with users managing servers and the associated costs of operation. Featherless provides the best of both worlds offering unmatched model range and variety but with serverless pricing.

Easy to use a wide variety of models with preferred providers Super easy integration into client SDKs $2 worth of Inference credits every month for PRO users

OpenAlex: Direct-On-Chip Hotspot Targeted Microjet Cooling

This paper investigates the thermal management strategies targeted at hotspot mitigation based on the Groq Language Processing Unit (LPU™) architecture. The customized heat transfer coefficient map can be achieved by tailoring the inlet nozzle diameter and pitch, as well as the flow conditions to match the hotspots within the chip.

Getting Started with Groq on Hugging Face

Read more about how to use Groq as an Inference Provider in its dedicated documentation page. See the list of supported models here.

Infra Spoke: Using Groq with Python and Hugging Face

This paper explores the concept of direct-on-chip hotspots targeted microjet cooling corresponding to the power map through the entire LPU™ chip. The study achieves a temperature nonuniformity within 4 ℃ across the logic die and significantly reduces the thermal coupling between LPU™ and the near

results.seo_title: Gemma 3n Open-Source Model Now Available
results.meta_description: Gemma 3n, a multimodal AI model, is now available on open-source libraries like transformers and timm.
results.body_html:
## Gemma 3n fully available in the open-source ecosystem!

Models released today Details of the models Architecture Highlights Performance & Benchmarks: Demo Space Inference with transformers Inference with pipeline Detailed inference with transformers Inference with MLX Inference with llama.cpp Inference with Transformers.js and ONNXRuntime Fine Tune in a Free Google Colab Hugging Face Gemma Recipes Conclusion Gemma 3n was announced as a preview during Google I/O. The on-device community got really excited, because this is a model designed from the ground up to run locally on your hardware. On top of that, it’s natively multimodal, supporting image, text, audio,

Today, Gemma 3n is finally available on the most used open source libraries. This includes transformers & timm, MLX, llama.cpp (text inputs), transformers.js, ollama, Google AI Edge, and others.

This post quickly goes through practical snippets to demonstrate how to use the model with these libraries, and how easy it is to fine-tune it for other domains.

Models released today

Here is the Gemma 3n Release Collection

Two model sizes have been released today, with two variants (base and instruct) each. The model names follow a non-standard nomenclature: they are called gemma-3n-E2B and gemma-3n-E4B. The E preceding the parameter count stands for Effective. Their actual parameter counts are 5B and 8B, respectively, but thanks to improvements in memory efficiency, they manage to only need 2B and 4B in VRAM (GPU memory).

These models, therefore, behave like 2B and 4B in terms of hardware support, but they punch over 2B/4B in terms of quality. The E2B model can run in as little as 2GB of GPU RAM, while E4B can run with just 3GB of GPU RAM.

Details of the models

In addition to the language decoder, Gemma 3n uses an audio encoder and a vision encoder. We highlight their main features below, and describe how they have been added to transformers and timm, as they are the reference for other implementations.

Vision Encoder (MobileNet-V5). Gemma 3n uses a new version of MobileNet: MobileNet-v5-300, which has been added to the new version of timm released today. Features 300M parameters.

Supports resolutions of 256x256, 512x512, and 768x768.

Achieves 60 FPS on Google Pixel, outperforming ViT Giant while using 3x fewer parameters.

Audio Encoder: Based on the Universal Speech Model (USM).

Enables speech-to-text and translation functionalities (e.g., English to Spanish/French).

Gemma 3n Architecture and Language Model. The architecture itself has been added to the new version of transformers released today. This implementation branches out to timm for image encoding, so there’s a single reference implementation of the MobileNet architecture.

Architecture Highlights

MatFormer Architecture: A nested transformer design, similar to Matryoshka embeddings, allows for various subsets of layers to be extracted as if they were individual models.

E2B and E4B were trained together, configuring E2B as a sub-model of E4B.

Users can “mix and match” layers, depending on their hardware characteristics and memory budget.

Per-Layer Embeddings (PLE): Reduces accelerator memory usage by offloading embeddings to the CPU. This is the reason why the E2B model, while having 5B real parameters, takes about as much GPU memory as if it were a 2B parameter model.

KV Cache Sharing: Accelerates long-context processing for audio and video, achieving 2x faster prefill compared to Gemma 3 4B.

Performance & Benchmarks:

LMArena Score: E4B is the first sub-10B model to achieve a score of 1300+.

source.title: Gemma 3n fully available in the open-source ecosystem!
source.summary: Gemma 3n fully available in the open-source ecosystem!

results.seo_title: Hugging Face's Production Infrastructure: Three Mighty Alerts
results.meta_description: Discover how Hugging Face's production infrastructure uses three mighty alerts to support its stability and scalability.
results.body_html:
The Hugging Face team has designed a robust monitoring and alerting system to ensure the stability and scalability of its platforms. This system is instrumental in identifying and responding to potential issues before they become major incidents.

High NAT Gateway ThroughputCentralizing egress traffic offers strategic visibility for comprehensive analysis.Cost optimization is key, with understanding pricing dynamics ensuring informed decisions on infrastructure configuration and architecture.A static threshold alert serves as an early warning system for unusual spikes in traffic, prompting regular review of traffic trends to stay on top of infrastructure growth and evolving needs.Hub Request Logs Archival Success RateComponentDescriptionFilebeatA lightweight log shipper collecting logs from application containers and forwarding them to the next stage of the pipeline.LogstashA powerful log processing tool applying mutations and transformations to incoming

logs, enriching them with GeoIP data for geolocation insights.ElasticsearchA distributed search and analytics engine storing logs in a scalable and flexible solution, allowing quick access for operational and troubleshooting purposes.Kubernetes API Request Errors and Rate Limiting Bonus Alert: New Cluster Sending Zero MetricsAutomated alerts help identify potential issues before they become major incidents, ensuring the stability and scalability of Hugging Face's platforms.New Cluster Sending Zero MetricsAvoiding private, low-cost paths between product-specific infrastructure can lead to increased costs.Leveraging DNS overrides to switch traffic through private network paths and public network paths

has become a valuable technique for cost savings.Configuration-as-code ensures the desired state is always in effect, but additional alerting layers provide an extra layer of protection against mistakes made when expressing the desired state through code.
target_keywords: Hub request logs archival success rate
Kubernetes API request errors and rate limiting

source.title: Three Mighty Alerts Supporting Hugging Face’s Production Infrastructure
source.summary: Three Mighty Alerts Supporting Hugging Face’s Production Infrastructure

results.seo_title: Reachy Mini: An Open-Source Robot for AI Builders of All Ages
results.meta_description: Discover the Reachy Mini, an open-source robot designed for human-robot interaction, creative coding, and AI experimentation.
results.body_html:
Direct answer to the title's core question: Reachy Mini is an open-source robot designed for human-robot interaction, creative coding, and AI experimentation.

Desktop-sized and starting from 299, ideal for creative prototyping, education and research. Priced from 299, making it an affordable option for developers, researchers, and educators.

15+ robot behaviorsProvided at launch on the Hugging Face hub, allowing users to play and learn right out of the box.

Hardware, software, and simulation environments fully open-source and community-supported. Stay tuned for the hardware release!

Fifth Infra Spoke: Community-Driven Development

Upload, share and download robot behaviors with the 10M users of the Hugging Face community. A platform for developers to collaborate and share their work.

results.seo_title: Hugging Face MCP Server: Streamable HTTP Transport and Remote Servers
results.meta_description: The Hugging Face Official MCP Server offers unique customization options for AI Assistants accessing the Hub, along with access to thousands of AI…
results.body_html:
The Hugging Face Official MCP Server offers unique customization options for AI Assistants accessing the Hub, along with access to thousands of AI applications through one simple URL.

Design Choices

  • Dynamic server configuration for users' tools on the fly
  • Simple and secure remote access via a single URL
  • Support for multiple transport options, including Streamable HTTP

Remote Servers

Transport Option Description
Streamable HTTP A modern and efficient transport option with low deployment resource overhead
SSE (Server Sent Events) A bi-directional transport option with a higher deployment resource overhead

Production Deployment

  • Stateless, Direct Response configuration for production
  • Lowest deployment resource overhead
  • Easy access to thousands of AI applications through a single URL

Tool List Change Notifications

Future support for real-time Tool List Changed notifications, but with practical challenges and limitations.

URL User Experience

  • Improved user experience with friendly instructions and a default response
  • Fixes issues with VSCode polling the endpoint multiple times per second

MCP Client Behaviour

The MCP Protocol sends several requests during initialization, including reading files, executing functions, and handling contextual prompts.

Infrastructure

  • Open-source code supports all variants of the Model Context Protocol
  • Streamable HTTP transport used for production deployment
  • Built-in observability dashboard for monitoring connections and tool list changes

source.title: Building the Hugging Face MCP Server
source.summary: Building the Hugging Face MCP Server
